prompt:
The speculative action cache in @prune/intelligence (bash read-classifier + freshness logic) has two safety holes:

1. Write-capable commands are classified as pure reads and become cacheable/servable: `find . -type f -delete` (deletes files) and `find . -type f -exec rm {} ;` (arbitrary execution) both pass the read classifier, and a newline-smuggled chain like "cat a\nrm b" sneaks past the shell-metacharacter screen. Caching a destructive op and replaying its recorded output instead of executing-and-observing is the worst failure this package can have.
2. Freshness for directory-scoped results uses a bare directory stat, which does not change when a file is edited IN PLACE (same mtime granularity, same entry count) — so a stale cached Grep/Glob result can be served after the underlying file content changed.

Make the classifier reject every write-capable form (redirects, pipes to mutators, command chains, command substitution, git-mutating subcommands, sed -i, rm/mv/cp/chmod/tee, uppercase or env-prefixed variants must fail safe) while continuing to accept genuine reads (git log/diff/show/status, cat/head/tail/ls/wc/grep/rg, find without action primaries). Make dir-scoped freshness detect in-place edits (e.g. derive the token from each matched file's path+mtime+size rather than one directory stat).
notes: Reference: tcrp harden F3 (write-capable command misclassification + freshness soundness) 2026-06-01.
